perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'Territory' from mainland China to Hong Kong introduces a contradiction. While both are in Asia, the laws governing content licensing can be drastically different, especially concerning censorship and distribution rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Copyright Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_citation: Article 2 of the Copyright Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Mainland China's copyright law is distinct from that of Hong Kong. By defining the territory as Hong Kong, the agreement might inadvertently create issues with content that is permissible in Hong Kong but restricted under mainland Chinese law, or vice versa. Specifically, Article 2 defines the scope of protection for copyright, which differs significantly between mainland China and Hong Kong.

(2) This Act shall also apply where an administrative decision is challenged by way of appeal or extraordinary appeal in an administrative authority, an appellate board or another comparable special authority. Section 2 — Scope restrictions (435/2003) (1) Sections 37—50 of this Act shall apply only to procedure in courts. (2) In other authorities, oral hearings, testimony and viewings shall be subject to sections 37, 38, 40, and 42 of the Administrative Procedure Act (434/2003). Section 3 — Precedence If another Act, or a Decree issued before the entry into force of this Act, contains provisions contrary to those in this Act, they shall apply instead of this Act. Chapter 2 — Right of appeal and appellate authority Section 4 — Right of appeal An administrative decision may be challenged by an appeal as provided in this Act. Section 5 — Admissibility (1) Any measure by which a case has been resolved or dismissed may be challenged by an appeal. perturbation.justification: The change in the definition of 'Territory' from mainland China to Hong Kong creates a legal contradiction because the laws governing content licensing differ significantly between the two regions, especially concerning censorship and distribution rights. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that mainland China's copyright law is distinct from that of Hong Kong, potentially leading to issues with content permissible in one but restricted in the other, contravening copyright protection scopes. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address China or Hong Kong copyright law, it shows that WIPO Lex provides laws and treaties of different jurisdictions and their scope (e.g. Administrative Judicial Procedure Act (Act No. 586/1996)). It provides an avenue to find laws that may apply in this case.

perturbation.original_text: Licensor hereby grants to Licensee a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual and non-perpetual license (subject to the duration for which Licensor has the rights to each Title as specified in Schedule A1-A5 of Schedule A) to: i. license, exhibit, distribute, reproduce, transmit, perform, display, and otherwise exploit and make available each Title within the Territory in any language by VOD (including SVOD, TVOD, AVOD and free VOD) for Internet, TV and mobile platforms (including, but not limited to, OTT streaming services, Sites and Mobile Sites)
perturbation.changed_text: Licensor hereby grants to Licensee a non-exclusive, royalty-free, license for 30 days to: i. license, exhibit, distribute, reproduce, transmit, perform, display, and otherwise exploit and make available each Title within the Territory in any language by VOD (including SVOD, TVOD, AVOD and free VOD) for Internet, TV and mobile platforms (including, but not limited to, OTT streaming services, Sites and Mobile Sites)
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'perpetual and non-perpetual' to 'license for 30 days' drastically alters the duration of the license, contradicting the initial intention of a potentially long-term agreement. This creates uncertainty regarding the actual term and enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_citation: Article 12 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 12 outlines the essential terms of a contract, including the term of performance. Reducing the licensing timeframe to 30 days from 'perpetual' creates a material change to the agreement’s core terms that could be challenged under contract law in the P.R.C.

Home User Management About Us Chinese lawinfochina.com > Laws & Regulations > Text Bookmark Download Print Search: "Fabao" Window Font Size: Circular or MOFTEC & CGA Concerning Authorizing Special Commissioner's Offices to Issue Automobiles Import Licenses to Chinese-Foreign EJV and CJV, the CGA's Control and Release of Automobiles Imported by WFOE and Other Related Issues (repealed) [Effective] 对外经济贸易部、海关总署关于授权特派员办事处签发中外合资、合作企业进口汽车许可证以及外资企业进口汽车由海关监管放行等有关问题的通知 [现行有效] 【法宝引证码】 Issuing authority： Instrumentalities of the State Council , All Ministries , Ministry of Foreign Trade & Economic Cooperation (incl. former Ministry of Foreign Economy & Trade) , All Agencies , General Administration of Customs Date issued： 02-15-1990 Effective date: 02-15-1990 Level of Authority: Departmental Regulatory Documents Area of Law: Foreign-funded Enterprises , Foreign Economy and Trade , Customs CIRCULAR OF THE MINISTRY OF FOREIGN ECONOMIC RELATIONS AND TRADE AND THE CUSTOMS GENERAL ADMINISTRATION CONCERNING AUTHORIZING SPECIAL COMMISSIONER'S OFFICES TO ISSUE AUTOMOBILES IMPORT LICENSES TO CHINESE-FOREIGN EQUITY JOINT VENTURES AND CONTRACTUAL JOINT VENTURES, THE CUSTOMS GENERAL ADMINISTRATION'S CONTROL AND RELEASE OF AUTOMOBILES IMPORTED BY WHOLLY FOREIGN-OWNED ENTERPRISES AND OTHER RELATED ISSUES. (Promulgated by the Ministry of Foreign Economic Relations & Trade and the Customs General Administration on February 15, 1990) 对外经济贸易部海关部署关于授权特派员办事处签发中外合资、合作企业进口汽车许可证以及外资企业进口汽车由海关监管放行等有关问题的通知 （１９９０年２月１５日发布） I. In accordance with the provisions of relevant laws and regulations concerning the enterprises with foreign investment and in the spirit of the Provisions of the State Council for the Encouragement of Foreign Investment , in order to Facilitate Chinese-foreign equity joint ventures and contractual joint ventures to go through formalities nearby for applying and obtaining import licenses for automobiles, it is decided upon the approval of the State Council to authorize all Special Commissioners' Offices on behalf of the Ministry of Foreign Economic Relations and Trade to issue import licenses for automobiles to the Chinese-foreign equity joint ventures and contractual joint ventures in the provinces, autonomous regions, municipalities directly under the central government and cities with separate plans within their respective business administered areas starting from March 1, 1990. The implementing procedures are as follows: 一、根据我国对外商投资企业的有关法律、法规的规定和《 国务院关于鼓励外商投资的规定 》的精神，为便利中外合资、合作企业就近办理早领进口汽车许可证手续，经国务院批准，现决定从１９９０年３月１日起，授权各特派员办事处代经贸部签发其业务辖区有关省、自治区、直辖市、、计划单列城市的中外合资、合作企业进口汽车许可证。具体实施办法如下： 1. The import licenses will be issued on the strength of verified document of allocation signed and sealed by the foreign economic relations and trade departments (commissions and bureaux) at the level of province (including city with separate plans) in accordance with the annual import quotes allocated by the Ministry of Foreign Economic Relations and Trade to each province, autonomous regions, municipality directly under the central government, city with separate plans, and the quotes will be at all times registered and canceled after verification. １、根据经贸部分配各省、自治区、直辖市、计划单列市的年度进口指标，验凭省级（含计划单列市）经贸厅、委（局）签章分配的证件签发进口许可证，并随时登记核销指标。 2. The quotas of a particular year shall only be used within that proper year. The licenses are to be issued from January 1 to December 31. No use beyond the proper year is permitted. ２、当年指标只限当年使用，发证日期从１月１日起到１２月３１日止。不得跨年度使用。 3. Export-oriented enterprises and technologically-advanced enterprises and other enterprises which need to import automobiles for implementing the export contract of product
perturbation.justification: The change from 'perpetual and non-perpetual' to 'license for 30 days' fundamentally alters the duration of the license granted by Licensor to Licensee. The original text implied a potentially long-term agreement. By reducing the license to 30 days, the changed text directly contradicts this. The Law Explanation states that this kind of change to the agreement’s core terms could be challenged under contract law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address contract terms, the Perturbation Explanation points out the uncertainty created by the altered term, making the contract questionable, which aligns with the Law Explanation provided.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall have the right to assign or sublicense any or all of its rights granted under this Agreement, in whole or in part, to third parties exhibiting the Titles in the ordinary course of Licensee's business with prior written notice to Licensor. Except as otherwise specified in the previous sentence, Licensee may not sublicense any of its rights under Section 2(a) without Licensor's prior written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld or delayed.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall have the right to assign or sublicense any or all of its rights granted under this Agreement, in whole or in part, to third parties exhibiting the Titles in the ordinary course of Licensee's business. Except as otherwise specified in the previous sentence, Licensee may not sublicense any of its rights under Section 2(a) without Licensor's consent, which may be withheld for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'with prior written notice to Licensor' and 'which shall not be unreasonably withheld or delayed' gives the Licensor the absolute right to deny sublicensing, even if reasonable, which is a significant deviation from typical contract law principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the contract is governed by New York law, UCC § 2-309 implies a requirement of reasonableness in commercial agreements. Removing the reasonableness constraint on withholding consent potentially violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in commercial contracts and introduces ambiguity regarding sublicensing rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for 'prior written notice' and allows the Licensor to withhold consent 'for any reason,' diverging from the principle of reasonableness implied in commercial agreements. The Law Explanation suggests a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on 'reasonable time' for performance, it highlights the UCC's emphasis on reasonableness in contract terms. The original text ensures sublicensing decisions are made with consideration for reasonableness and notification. By removing these stipulations, the changed text introduces a potential for arbitrary denial of sublicensing, arguably contradicting the UCC's underlying principle of good faith, and falling under 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' as the deviation makes the contract unenforceable under typical contract law principles.
